text.title: MCP Server Implementation — TypeScript and Python — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules that gate any application of the mcp-server-implementation methodology. Sources: Anthropic — Model Context Protocol specification 2025; GitHub: modelcontextprotocol/typescript-sdk; GitHub: modelcontextprotocol/python-sdk.

rule.statement: Declare server capabilities matching exactly what the server implements (tools, resources, prompts with listChanged/subscribe flags); never advertise a capability the server cannot serve.
rule.rationale: Clients gate UI and request routing on advertised capabilities; mismatched capabilities cause undefined client behaviour and lost requests.

rule.statement: Tool definitions require a unique name (1–128 chars), a clear human description, and a valid JSON Schema inputSchema (type:object); optional outputSchema when the tool returns structured payloads.
rule.rationale: The LLM selects tools based on the description; vague names cause wrong tool selection and silent task failure.

rule.statement: Return `isError: true` for tool execution failures with an actionable error message; never raise an unhandled exception from the tool handler.
rule.rationale: Unhandled exceptions break the agent loop; the `isError` flag lets the model read the failure and self-correct.

rule.statement: Use stdio transport for single-client local servers and HTTP+SSE for multi-client or remote servers; never expose HTTP transport without bearer-token auth.
rule.rationale: stdio trusts the local process; HTTP needs explicit auth or the server becomes an open RPC endpoint on the network.

rule.statement: Keep individual tool responses under 10 KB and log every tool invocation (caller id, inputs, outputs) for audit.
rule.rationale: Large responses eat context window and slow the agent loop; audit logs are mandatory for any tool that writes state.
